name: Elmer Bernstein
abstract: Elmer Bernstein ( BURN-steen; April 4, 1922 – August 18, 2004) was an American composer and conductor. In a career that spanned over five decades, he composed "some of the most recognizable and memorable themes in Hollywood history", including over 150 original film scores, as well as scores for nearly 80 television productions.

about_quotes.speaker: John Altman
about_quotes.evidence: I orchestrated for Elmer Bernstein on the film... I love working with him
about_quotes.doc_title: John Altman interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, September 14, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Schuller Gunther
about_quotes.evidence: Elmer Bernstein is mentioned only as part of a group ('George Roy Hill, Elmer Bernstein, me') discussing the film score, with no personal interaction or relationship described between Schuller and Bernstein.
about_quotes.doc_title: Schuller Gunther — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Holman Bill
about_quotes.evidence: In December of ‟55 in Los Angeles, Elmer Bernstein‟s The Man with the Golden Arm, the soundtrack for the film. Was that a memorable experience, working in that context? ... Yeah, it was.
about_quotes.doc_title: Holman Bill — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: John LaBarbera
about_quotes.evidence: there’s a Dixieland section in there that Elmer Bernstein had in the film. He cut the entire thing out.

about_quotes.speaker: Pete Candoli
about_quotes.evidence: I’ve done so many motion pictures with all composers... 'Man with the Golden Arm' with Elmer Bernstein
about_quotes.doc_title: Pete Candoli interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] And Elmer Bernstein’s Man with the Golden Arm. [INTERVIEWEE] Exactly, um-hm.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
